name: Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation (XSS)
description: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) occurs when software does not neutralize or incorrectly neutralizes user-controllable input before it is placed in output that is served as a web page to other users. Attackers inject malicious scripts (typically JavaScript) into web pages viewed by other users. There are three main variants: Reflected XSS where the malicious script is reflected off a web server in error messages or search results; Stored XSS where the injected script is permanently stored on target servers in databases, message forums, or comment fields; and DOM-based XSS where the vulnerability exists in client-side scripts that modify the DOM. Successful exploitation can lead to session hijacking via cookie theft, website defacement, redirection to malicious sites, keylogging, and complete account takeover. XSS is one of the most prevalent web application vulnerabilities and underpins numerous attack chains.

name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command (SQL Injection)
description: SQL Injection occurs when software constructs all or part of an SQL command using externally-influenced input from an upstream component without properly neutralizing special elements that could modify the intended SQL command. Attackers craft input containing SQL syntax that alters query logic, allowing them to read, modify, or delete database contents, execute administrative operations, and in some cases execute operating system commands. Attack variants include classic in-band injection where results appear directly in the application response, blind injection where attackers infer information from application behavior or timing differences, and out-of-band injection where data is exfiltrated through DNS or HTTP requests. SQLi enables full database compromise, authentication bypass, data exfiltration, and can serve as a pivot point to compromise the underlying server through techniques like xp_cmdshell on MSSQL or LOAD_FILE on MySQL.

name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command (OS Command Injection)
description: OS Command Injection occurs when software constructs operating system commands using externally-influenced input without neutralizing special elements such as semicolons, pipes, backticks, or command substitution characters. Attackers inject arbitrary OS commands that execute with the privileges of the vulnerable application, potentially achieving remote code execution on the underlying server. Common injection points include web application parameters passed to system(), exec(), or popen() functions, file processing utilities, network diagnostic tools, and any functionality that shells out to the operating system. Successful exploitation gives attackers the ability to read and write arbitrary files, establish reverse shells, install backdoors, pivot to internal networks, and exfiltrate sensitive data. This is particularly dangerous when the application runs with elevated privileges such as root or SYSTEM.

name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in a Command (Command Injection)
description: Command Injection is a broader category encompassing situations where software constructs a command string using externally-influenced input and passes it to an interpreter without proper neutralization. Unlike OS Command Injection (CWE-78), this applies to any command interpreter including database shells, scripting engines, mail servers (SMTP injection), and LDAP interpreters. Attackers manipulate the command structure by injecting delimiter characters, escape sequences, or additional commands. The impact depends on the target interpreter but generally includes unauthorized data access, data modification, and potentially arbitrary code execution within the interpreter context. Prevention requires parameterized APIs, input validation against strict allowlists, and avoiding string concatenation when building commands.

name: Improper Control of Generation of Code (Code Injection)
description: Code Injection occurs when software allows externally-influenced input to be used in the generation or modification of code that is subsequently executed by the application. This includes eval() injection in languages like Python, JavaScript, Ruby, and PHP; template injection in template engines (Jinja2, Twig, Freemarker); and expression language injection in frameworks like Spring EL or OGNL. Attackers can execute arbitrary code within the application context, gaining full control over the application logic, accessing sensitive data, reading files from the server filesystem, and potentially achieving remote code execution on the host system. This weakness is distinct from OS Command Injection as the injected code runs within the application runtime rather than through a system shell, but the impact can be equally severe.

name: Improper Limitation of a Pathname to a Restricted Directory (Path Traversal)
description: Path Traversal (also known as Directory Traversal) occurs when software uses external input to construct a pathname intended to identify a file or directory within a restricted parent directory, but fails to properly neutralize special elements like ../ sequences that can resolve to a location outside that directory. Attackers traverse the directory structure to read sensitive files such as /etc/passwd, /etc/shadow, application configuration files containing database credentials, private keys, or source code. In write scenarios, attackers can overwrite critical system files, plant web shells, or modify application configuration. Variants include absolute path traversal, relative path traversal, and null-byte injection to truncate file extensions. Both Unix-style (../) and Windows-style (..\) traversal sequences must be considered, along with URL-encoded and double-encoded variants.

name: Unrestricted Upload of File with Dangerous Type
description: This weakness occurs when software allows users to upload files without sufficient restrictions on file type, content, or storage location. Attackers upload malicious files such as web shells (PHP, JSP, ASPX), executable binaries, HTML files containing scripts, SVG files with embedded JavaScript, or polyglot files that pass type validation but execute as code. Even with extension validation, attackers bypass controls using double extensions (shell.php.jpg), null bytes (shell.php%00.jpg), MIME type manipulation, or by exploiting inconsistencies between validation and execution. Successful exploitation typically leads to remote code execution on the server, especially when uploaded files are stored in web-accessible directories. The impact ranges from website defacement and data theft to complete server compromise and use as a staging point for further attacks.

name: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)
description: SSRF occurs when a web application fetches a remote resource using a URL that is wholly or partially supplied by an attacker, without validating that the URL points to an expected destination. Attackers exploit this to make the server send requests to internal services, cloud metadata endpoints (169.254.169.254), internal APIs, databases, and other infrastructure that is not directly accessible from the internet. Common attack scenarios include reading cloud instance credentials from metadata services (leading to full cloud account compromise), port scanning internal networks, accessing internal admin panels, exploiting trust relationships between internal services, and bypassing network-level access controls. SSRF payloads often leverage protocol handlers (file://, gopher://, dict://), DNS rebinding, or IP address obfuscation techniques (decimal, hex, IPv6) to bypass allowlist/blocklist filters.

name: Improper Restriction of XML External Entity Reference (XXE)
description: XXE (XML External Entity) injection occurs when software processes XML input containing a reference to an external entity, and the XML parser is configured to resolve external entities. Attackers craft malicious XML documents with DTD declarations that reference local files (file:///etc/passwd), internal network resources (http://internal-server/), or trigger denial of service through recursive entity expansion (Billion Laughs attack). XXE can lead to disclosure of confidential data, server-side request forgery, port scanning of internal networks, remote code execution in some configurations (via expect:// protocol handler in PHP), and denial of service. Blind XXE variants use out-of-band techniques where data is exfiltrated via DNS lookups or HTTP requests to attacker-controlled servers. Prevention requires disabling external entity resolution and DTD processing in XML parsers.

name: Deserialization of Untrusted Data
description: Insecure deserialization occurs when software deserializes data from untrusted sources without sufficient validation, allowing attackers to manipulate serialized objects to achieve malicious outcomes. In languages like Java, PHP, Python, Ruby, and .NET, attackers craft serialized payloads that exploit gadget chains (sequences of method calls triggered during deserialization) to achieve remote code execution. Java deserialization attacks using Apache Commons Collections, Spring, or other library gadgets are particularly devastating and have led to major breaches. Beyond RCE, insecure deserialization can enable authentication bypass, privilege escalation, injection attacks, and denial of service. The vulnerability is especially dangerous because exploitation often requires no authentication and can be fully automated. Known exploitation frameworks like ysoserial (Java) and phpggc (PHP) provide ready-made payloads for common library gadget chains.

name: Buffer Copy without Checking Size of Input (Classic Buffer Overflow)
description: Classic buffer overflow occurs when software copies data into a buffer without first checking that the size of the data does not exceed the size of the buffer, causing data to overflow into adjacent memory. In stack-based overflows, attackers overwrite the return address to redirect execution to injected shellcode or ROP gadgets. Heap-based overflows corrupt heap metadata or adjacent heap objects to achieve arbitrary write primitives. Buffer overflows have historically been among the most exploited vulnerabilities, enabling remote code execution in applications written in memory-unsafe languages like C and C++. Modern protections like ASLR, DEP/NX, stack canaries, and CFI raise the exploitation bar but do not eliminate the risk, as techniques like information leaks, partial overwrites, and JIT spraying continue to evolve.

name: Out-of-bounds Write
description: Out-of-bounds write occurs when software writes data past the end, or before the beginning, of the intended buffer. This is a generalization of buffer overflow that includes writes at arbitrary offsets rather than just sequential overwrites. Exploitation can corrupt adjacent data structures, modify program control flow, or overwrite security-critical values like function pointers, vtable entries, or authentication flags. Out-of-bounds writes frequently appear in image parsers, media codecs, font rendering engines, and file format handlers where complex data structures are processed. This weakness consistently ranks at or near the top of the CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Weaknesses due to its prevalence and the severity of exploitation outcomes, which typically include remote code execution with the privileges of the vulnerable application.

name: Out-of-bounds Read
description: Out-of-bounds read occurs when software reads data past the end or before the beginning of the intended buffer, potentially exposing sensitive information from adjacent memory. While typically less severe than out-of-bounds writes, these vulnerabilities can leak stack or heap contents including cryptographic keys, authentication tokens, private data, and memory layout information that defeats ASLR. The infamous Heartbleed vulnerability (CVE-2014-0160) in OpenSSL was an out-of-bounds read that exposed private keys and session data from server memory. Out-of-bounds reads can also cause crashes (denial of service) if they access unmapped memory pages, and the leaked information often serves as a prerequisite for more complex exploitation chains.

name: Use After Free
description: Use-after-free occurs when software continues to reference memory after it has been freed, allowing an attacker who controls the freed memory contents to influence program behavior. When the freed memory is reallocated for a different purpose, the dangling reference may read attacker-controlled data or allow corruption of the new allocation. Exploitation typically involves heap spraying or targeted allocation to place controlled data at the freed memory location, then triggering the dangling reference to achieve code execution. Use-after-free vulnerabilities are particularly common in browsers (JavaScript engine object handling), PDF readers, and other complex applications with intricate object lifecycle management. They are a primary target for modern exploit development and frequently appear in exploit chains used by advanced threat actors.

name: Integer Overflow or Wraparound
description: Integer overflow occurs when an arithmetic operation produces a value that exceeds the maximum (or falls below the minimum) value for the data type, causing the value to wrap around. Attackers exploit integer overflows to bypass size checks that guard buffer allocations (leading to heap buffer overflows), corrupt loop counters (causing infinite loops or denial of service), or manipulate financial calculations. A classic pattern involves overflowing a size calculation so a small buffer is allocated, followed by a copy operation using the original (large) size, resulting in a heap buffer overflow. Integer overflows in signed arithmetic can also produce negative values from positive operands, subverting comparisons and control flow. These vulnerabilities are particularly insidious because the overflow occurs silently in most languages without runtime errors.

name: NULL Pointer Dereference
description: NULL pointer dereference occurs when software attempts to use a pointer that is expected to be valid but is NULL, typically causing an application crash (denial of service). While traditionally considered a reliability rather than security issue, NULL pointer dereferences can be security-relevant in certain contexts: on systems without memory protection at address zero, attackers may map memory at the NULL page and achieve code execution; in multi-threaded applications, a controlled crash may corrupt shared state; and in kernel code, NULL dereferences have historically been exploitable for privilege escalation. The vulnerability commonly arises from missing return value checks, error handling omissions, and race conditions that clear pointers unexpectedly.

name: Improper Restriction of Operations within the Bounds of a Memory Buffer
description: This is the parent category for all buffer-related vulnerabilities where software performs read or write operations without ensuring they stay within the boundaries of the allocated memory buffer. It encompasses buffer overflows (CWE-120), out-of-bounds reads (CWE-125), out-of-bounds writes (CWE-787), and related memory safety issues. These weaknesses are the foundation of memory corruption exploitation in languages lacking automatic bounds checking (primarily C and C++). Exploitation consequences range from information disclosure and denial of service to arbitrary code execution. Memory safety vulnerabilities remain the single largest class of critical security bugs in major software projects including operating systems, browsers, and network infrastructure, driving industry adoption of memory-safe languages like Rust and Go.

name: Improper Input Validation
description: Improper input validation is a broad weakness category where software receives input but does not validate (or incorrectly validates) that the input conforms to expected properties before using it in security-sensitive operations. This encompasses failures to validate data type, length, range, format, permitted characters, and business logic constraints. It is the root cause enabling numerous more specific vulnerabilities including injection attacks, buffer overflows, path traversals, and denial of service. Effective input validation requires defining strict allowlists of acceptable input, validating all input at trust boundaries (user input, API parameters, file uploads, environment variables), using appropriate validation for the context (encoding for HTML output, parameterization for SQL), and implementing defense in depth with validation at multiple layers.

name: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor
description: This weakness occurs when software exposes sensitive information to an actor who is not explicitly authorized to have access to that information. Information exposure can occur through many channels: error messages revealing stack traces, database schemas, or internal paths; API responses returning more data than necessary (over-fetching); debug endpoints left active in production; logs containing credentials or PII; HTTP headers exposing server versions and technology stack; and timing side channels leaking information about internal state. The exposed information frequently serves as reconnaissance data enabling more targeted attacks, including credential harvesting, privilege escalation, and exploitation of identified software versions with known vulnerabilities.

name: Generation of Error Message Containing Sensitive Information
description: This weakness occurs when software generates error messages that include sensitive information about the application environment, users, or associated data. Detailed error messages in production may expose database query structures (aiding SQL injection), file system paths (aiding path traversal), stack traces (revealing framework versions and code structure), internal IP addresses (mapping internal network), or user data (violating privacy). Attackers systematically trigger errors through malformed input, invalid parameters, and edge cases to harvest this information for subsequent attacks. Best practice requires generic error messages in production with detailed errors logged server-side only, separate error handling for development and production environments, and custom error pages that do not reveal internal details.

name: Insertion of Sensitive Information into Log File
description: This weakness occurs when software writes sensitive information such as passwords, API keys, session tokens, credit card numbers, or PII into log files that may be accessible to unauthorized actors. Log files are frequently stored with broader access permissions than application data, transmitted to centralized logging systems with different access controls, retained for extended periods, and included in support bundles or diagnostic exports. Attackers who gain read access to logs (through log management platforms, backup files, or LFI vulnerabilities) can harvest credentials and tokens for account takeover, or PII for regulatory violations. This weakness is particularly common in applications that log full HTTP request/response bodies, database queries with parameters, or exception details including variable values.

name: Cleartext Storage of Sensitive Information
description: This weakness occurs when software stores sensitive information in cleartext within a resource that might be accessible to unauthorized actors. Sensitive data includes passwords, cryptographic keys, API tokens, PII, financial data, and health records. Common cleartext storage locations include configuration files, flat-file databases, application preferences, registry entries, environment variables baked into container images, and mobile device local storage. Attackers who gain filesystem access (through path traversal, backup exposure, or system compromise) can immediately read all stored secrets without needing to defeat any cryptographic protections. Prevention requires encrypting sensitive data at rest using strong algorithms, storing credentials in dedicated secret management systems (HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault), and using one-way hashing for passwords.

name: Cleartext Transmission of Sensitive Information
description: This weakness occurs when software transmits sensitive information across a network in cleartext without encryption, making it susceptible to interception by any actor with network access along the communication path. Protocols like HTTP, FTP, TELNET, SMTP (without STARTTLS), and unencrypted database connections expose credentials, session tokens, personal data, and business-critical information to network sniffing attacks. Attackers on the same network segment, at ISP level, or on compromised network infrastructure can passively capture this data using tools like Wireshark, tcpdump, or Responder. In cloud environments, this is particularly dangerous as traffic may traverse shared infrastructure. Prevention requires TLS/SSL encryption for all channels carrying sensitive data, with proper certificate validation and strong cipher suite configuration.

name: Use of a Broken or Risky Cryptographic Algorithm
description: This weakness occurs when software uses a cryptographic algorithm that is known to be weak, broken, or inappropriate for the security requirements. Examples include DES and 3DES for symmetric encryption (replaced by AES), MD5 and SHA-1 for integrity verification or password hashing (vulnerable to collision attacks), RC4 for stream encryption (multiple biases discovered), and RSA with small key sizes or without proper padding (PKCS#1 v1.5 vulnerable to Bleichenbacher attacks). Attackers can exploit broken algorithms to forge signatures, decrypt communications, crack password hashes, or bypass integrity checks. Cryptographic agility (the ability to swap algorithms without code changes) is essential as algorithm weaknesses are continually discovered. Standards organizations like NIST publish recommended algorithms and key lengths that should be followed.

name: Use of Weak Hash
description: This weakness occurs when software uses a hashing algorithm that does not provide sufficient collision resistance, preimage resistance, or computational cost for its intended purpose. Using MD5 or SHA-1 for digital signatures or certificate validation is dangerous because practical collision attacks exist. Using fast hash functions (MD5, SHA-256) for password hashing is inappropriate because they can be brute-forced at billions of attempts per second on modern GPUs. Password hashing requires purpose-built algorithms like bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2 that incorporate salting and adjustable work factors. For integrity verification, SHA-256 or SHA-3 should be used as minimum standards. The distinction between hash function quality and appropriate use context is critical in cryptographic engineering.

name: Use of Insufficiently Random Values
description: This weakness occurs when software uses insufficiently random numbers in a security context that requires unpredictability. Common sources of weak randomness include language-default PRNGs (Math.random() in JavaScript, random.random() in Python, java.util.Random) which are designed for statistical simulation rather than security and have predictable output given seed recovery. When weak randomness is used for session tokens, CSRF tokens, password reset links, cryptographic keys, or OTPs, attackers can predict or brute-force the generated values to hijack sessions, forge tokens, or compromise cryptographic operations. Secure alternatives include /dev/urandom, CryptGenRandom, SecureRandom, and the secrets module in Python, which draw from OS-provided cryptographic entropy sources.

name: Use of Cryptographically Weak Pseudo-Random Number Generator
description: This weakness specifically addresses the use of PRNGs that are not designed for cryptographic applications in security-sensitive contexts. Non-cryptographic PRNGs like Mersenne Twister (used by default in many languages), Linear Congruential Generators, and XorShift have mathematical properties that make their output predictable given sufficient observed values. An attacker who can observe as few as 624 consecutive 32-bit outputs from Mersenne Twister can reconstruct the full internal state and predict all future outputs. This enables prediction of session tokens, nonces, password reset codes, and other security-critical random values. Always use cryptographically secure PRNGs (CSPRNGs) that are designed to resist state recovery and output prediction attacks.

name: Inadequate Encryption Strength
description: This weakness occurs when software uses encryption with key lengths, modes, or parameters that do not provide sufficient security strength for the sensitivity of the protected data. Examples include RSA keys shorter than 2048 bits, AES keys shorter than 128 bits, Diffie-Hellman parameters using common primes or insufficient bit lengths, and elliptic curve keys on weak curves. Advances in computing power, including quantum computing threats, continually raise the minimum acceptable key lengths. Current recommendations include AES-256 for symmetric encryption, RSA-2048 minimum (RSA-4096 preferred) or ECDSA P-256+ for asymmetric operations, and SHA-256+ for hashing. Organizations should plan for post-quantum cryptographic migration as quantum computers capable of breaking current asymmetric algorithms advance.

name: Improper Certificate Validation
description: This weakness occurs when software does not properly validate SSL/TLS certificates during secure communications, enabling man-in-the-middle attacks. Common validation failures include disabling certificate verification entirely (verify=False in Python requests), not checking certificate expiration, not validating the hostname matches the certificate CN/SAN, accepting self-signed certificates in production, not checking certificate revocation status (CRL/OCSP), and trusting overly broad certificate authorities. Attackers on the network path can present their own certificates and intercept all encrypted traffic, capturing credentials, session tokens, API keys, and sensitive data. This is particularly prevalent in mobile applications and internal service-to-service communications where developers disable validation during development and fail to re-enable it for production.

name: Use of Hard-coded Credentials
description: This weakness occurs when software contains hard-coded credentials (passwords, API keys, cryptographic keys, tokens) embedded directly in source code, configuration files shipped with the application, or compiled binaries. Hard-coded credentials are discoverable through reverse engineering, source code review, or scanning public repositories (many secrets are accidentally committed to GitHub). Once discovered, these credentials provide persistent unauthorized access that cannot be easily revoked without releasing a software update. This weakness is especially dangerous in IoT devices, firmware, and distributed software where updates are difficult. Hard-coded credentials in backend services enable attackers to bypass authentication entirely. Prevention requires externalized secret management, environment-variable-based configuration, and automated secret scanning in CI/CD pipelines.

name: Use of Hard-coded Password
description: This is a specific instance of hard-coded credentials where a password is embedded directly in software source code or binaries for inbound authentication (allowing anyone who discovers it to gain access) or outbound authentication (connecting to external services). Hard-coded passwords in firmware, IoT devices, and network equipment have been widely exploited in botnets (Mirai) and targeted attacks. The password is identical across all installations, meaning one compromise exposes all deployments. Even if the password is obfuscated or encrypted with a hard-coded key, determined attackers can recover it through reverse engineering. Organizations should use unique per-instance credentials, certificate-based authentication, or credential management services instead.

name: Use of Hard-coded Cryptographic Key
description: This weakness occurs when software uses a hard-coded cryptographic key for encryption, decryption, signing, or authentication. Since the key is identical across all installations and discoverable through reverse engineering or source code access, any data encrypted with it can be decrypted by anyone who extracts the key. Similarly, digital signatures created with a hard-coded private key can be forged. This weakness compromises the fundamental assumption of cryptography that keys are secret. Common occurrences include hard-coded AES keys for encrypting local data, hard-coded HMAC secrets for signing JWTs or API requests, and embedded TLS private keys in firmware. Proper key management requires generating unique keys per deployment, storing them in HSMs or key management services, and implementing key rotation procedures.

name: Improper Authentication
description: This broad weakness category covers situations where software does not properly prove that an actor claiming a particular identity is actually that identity. This includes missing authentication for critical functions, use of easily bypassed authentication schemes, improper implementation of authentication protocols, failure to re-authenticate for sensitive operations, and authentication that can be circumvented by manipulating client-side state. Attackers exploit improper authentication to impersonate legitimate users, access administrative functionality, access other users data, and perform unauthorized operations. Proper authentication requires strong credential verification, multi-factor authentication for sensitive operations, secure session management, protection against credential stuffing and brute force, and proper handling of authentication edge cases.

name: Missing Authentication for Critical Function
description: This weakness occurs when software does not require any authentication for functionality that should require a provable user identity or that accesses sensitive data. Common examples include administrative endpoints accessible without login, API endpoints that bypass authentication middleware, debug or diagnostic interfaces exposed in production, file upload/download functions without access controls, and configuration modification endpoints. Attackers discover these unprotected functions through directory brute-forcing, API enumeration, source code review, or by observing client-side code that references the endpoints. The impact is typically severe since critical functions often manage user data, system configuration, or administrative operations. Prevention requires implementing authentication at the framework level with a default-deny approach.

name: Improper Restriction of Excessive Authentication Attempts
description: This weakness occurs when software does not implement sufficient measures to prevent or detect brute-force authentication attacks. Without rate limiting, account lockout, CAPTCHA challenges, or progressive delays, attackers can systematically try large numbers of credential combinations against login endpoints. Modern brute-force tools can test millions of password combinations per hour against web applications, and credential stuffing attacks using breached password lists are highly effective given widespread password reuse. Effective countermeasures include progressive account lockout with backoff, IP-based rate limiting, CAPTCHA after failed attempts, multi-factor authentication, monitoring for distributed brute-force patterns, and device fingerprinting to identify suspicious login behavior.

name: Session Fixation
description: Session fixation occurs when software does not regenerate the session identifier after a successful authentication, allowing an attacker who knows or sets the session ID before authentication to hijack the authenticated session. The attacker sets the victim session ID to a known value (via URL parameter, cookie injection, or cross-site scripting), waits for the victim to authenticate, and then uses the known session ID to access the authenticated session. This differs from session hijacking where an existing valid session is stolen. Prevention is straightforward: always regenerate the session identifier upon successful authentication, privilege level changes, and other security state transitions. Most modern web frameworks provide session regeneration functions, but developers must ensure they are called at the appropriate points.

name: Insufficient Session Expiration
description: This weakness occurs when software does not sufficiently limit the lifetime of session tokens, allowing authenticated sessions to remain valid for extended periods. Long-lived sessions increase the window during which stolen session tokens (via XSS, network sniffing, or physical access) remain useful to attackers. Sessions that never expire or have extremely long timeouts enable persistent unauthorized access even after the legitimate user has finished their work. Effective session management requires absolute session timeouts (maximum session lifetime regardless of activity), idle timeouts (inactivity-based expiration), session invalidation upon password change, proper server-side session termination on logout, and limiting concurrent sessions per user for sensitive applications.

name: Weak Password Recovery Mechanism for Forgotten Password
description: This weakness occurs when the password recovery mechanism can be exploited to gain unauthorized access to user accounts. Weak recovery mechanisms include security questions with easily guessable or researched answers, password reset links sent to unverified email addresses, reset tokens that are predictable or insufficiently random, reset links that do not expire, lack of rate limiting on reset requests, and information leakage confirming whether accounts exist. Attackers target password reset flows as an alternative to brute-forcing login credentials, exploiting the weaker authentication requirements of the recovery process. Secure recovery requires cryptographically random time-limited tokens, notification to the registered email/phone, re-validation of identity through MFA, and rate limiting of recovery requests.

name: Weak Password Requirements
description: This weakness occurs when software does not enforce adequate password complexity or length requirements, making user passwords vulnerable to brute-force and dictionary attacks. Weak requirements include allowing very short passwords (under 8 characters), not checking against known breached password lists, not requiring character diversity, and not implementing password history to prevent reuse. NIST SP 800-63B recommends a minimum of 8 characters with a maximum of at least 64 characters, checking against breached password databases, and avoiding arbitrary complexity rules (uppercase, special character requirements) in favor of length-based strength. Password strength meters and real-time feedback help users choose stronger passwords.

name: Insufficiently Protected Credentials
description: This weakness occurs when software does not adequately protect user credentials during storage, transmission, or processing. This encompasses multiple sub-weaknesses including storing passwords in plaintext or reversible encryption, transmitting credentials over unencrypted channels, logging credentials in application logs, caching credentials in browser or proxy caches, and exposing credentials through client-side storage (localStorage, cookies without secure flag). Attackers who obtain insufficiently protected credentials gain direct access to user accounts and any systems those credentials protect. Given widespread password reuse, compromised credentials from one service frequently enable access to many other services. Defense-in-depth requires hashing passwords with bcrypt/argon2, TLS for all credential transmission, credential-free authentication where possible (tokens, certificates), and secure credential management practices.

name: Plaintext Storage of a Password
description: This weakness occurs when software stores user passwords in plaintext without applying any cryptographic transformation, meaning anyone who gains access to the storage medium (database, file, backup) can immediately read all passwords. This is among the most basic security failures an application can have, yet it continues to be discovered in production systems. A database breach of a system storing plaintext passwords exposes every user credential immediately, and due to password reuse, typically leads to compromise of users accounts across multiple services. Passwords must always be stored using purposefully slow, salted, one-way hashing algorithms like bcrypt, scrypt, or Argon2id with appropriate work factors that are increased over time as computing power grows.

name: Use of Password Hash With Insufficient Computational Effort
description: This weakness occurs when software hashes passwords using algorithms that can be computed too quickly, enabling practical brute-force attacks against the hashed values. Fast hash functions like MD5, SHA-1, SHA-256, and even HMAC variants can be computed at billions of iterations per second on modern GPUs with tools like hashcat, making it feasible to recover most user passwords from their hashes. Purpose-built password hashing algorithms like bcrypt, scrypt, and Argon2id incorporate adjustable work factors (time cost, memory cost, parallelism parameters) that make each hash computation deliberately expensive, dramatically reducing the rate at which an attacker can test candidate passwords. Work factors should be tuned so that hashing takes at least 100ms per password and should be increased over time.

name: Missing Authorization
description: This weakness occurs when software does not perform any authorization check when an actor attempts to access a resource or perform an action that should require verified permissions. Unlike authentication failures (verifying identity), authorization failures involve letting authenticated users access resources or actions beyond their permitted scope. Common examples include API endpoints that check authentication but not role-based permissions, direct object references without ownership verification, administrative functions accessible to regular users, and horizontal privilege escalation where User A can access User B data. Missing authorization is distinct from incorrect authorization (CWE-863) because no check is performed at all. Prevention requires implementing authorization at every access point with a default-deny policy and consistent enforcement across all application layers.

name: Incorrect Authorization
description: This weakness occurs when software performs an authorization check but does so incorrectly, allowing actors to access resources or perform actions beyond their intended permissions. Unlike missing authorization where no check exists, incorrect authorization involves flawed logic in existing checks. Examples include checking the wrong permission, using client-controlled data for authorization decisions, relying on security through obscurity (hidden URLs), implementing allow-by-default logic, failing to check authorization for all code paths to a resource, and race conditions in permission checks (TOCTOU). Incorrect authorization vulnerabilities are often subtle and survive code review because authorization logic exists but contains logical flaws. Centralized authorization services, comprehensive access control testing, and formal authorization models help prevent these issues.

name: Improper Authorization
description: This is the parent category for authorization weaknesses where software does not properly restrict access to resources or functionality from unauthorized actors. It encompasses both missing authorization checks (CWE-862) and incorrect authorization logic (CWE-863). Improper authorization is consistently rated as one of the most critical and prevalent web application vulnerabilities, corresponding to OWASP A01:2021 Broken Access Control. The impact is typically severe because authorization failures directly enable access to other users data, administrative functions, or restricted resources. Proper authorization requires a centralized, well-tested authorization mechanism that applies consistently across all application endpoints, with default-deny semantics and the principle of least privilege applied to all roles and permissions.

name: Improper Privilege Management
description: This weakness occurs when software does not properly assign, modify, track, or check privileges for actors, resulting in some actors having more privileges than required or gaining privileges they should not have. This includes granting administrative rights by default, failing to revoke temporary privileges, not implementing the principle of least privilege, allowing privilege accumulation over time, and insecure delegation of privileges. Improper privilege management creates unnecessarily large attack surfaces because compromising any over-privileged account provides access far beyond what the account needs. Prevention requires implementing role-based access control (RBAC) or attribute-based access control (ABAC), regular privilege audits, just-in-time privilege elevation, and documentation of the minimum privileges required for each role.

name: Execution with Unnecessary Privileges
description: This weakness occurs when software executes with higher privileges than necessary to perform its required functions. Running applications as root/SYSTEM, granting database accounts DBA privileges when only SELECT is needed, or deploying cloud services with admin IAM roles are common examples. If such an application is compromised through any vulnerability, the attacker inherits these excessive privileges, greatly amplifying the impact. A web application running as root means any code execution vulnerability immediately gives the attacker full system control. Prevention requires applying the principle of least privilege: running services as dedicated low-privilege accounts, using minimal database permissions, assigning narrow IAM roles, and dropping privileges after initialization if elevated privileges were needed for startup.

name: Incorrect Permission Assignment for Critical Resource
description: This weakness occurs when software sets permissions on a critical resource (configuration files, cryptographic keys, databases, log files, executables) in a way that allows unintended actors to read, modify, or execute it. Common examples include world-readable configuration files containing database passwords, writable directories for application binaries allowing replacement, overly permissive ACLs on cloud storage buckets, and shared temp directories used for sensitive intermediate files. Attackers exploit incorrect permissions to steal credentials, modify application behavior, inject malicious code, or access sensitive data. This is particularly common in deployment and installation scripts that set permissions too broadly for convenience. Prevention requires following the principle of least privilege for file permissions and regularly auditing permissions on critical resources.

name: Incorrect Default Permissions
description: This weakness occurs when software is installed with default permissions that are more permissive than necessary, exposing resources to unauthorized access from the moment of deployment. Default configurations are often designed for ease of installation and compatibility rather than security, resulting in overly permissive file permissions, open network ports, enabled debug features, and anonymous access. Many major breaches have resulted from default configurations, including publicly accessible Elasticsearch instances, MongoDB databases with no authentication, and S3 buckets with public read access. The OWASP Security Misconfiguration category (A05:2021) directly addresses this class of issues. Organizations must establish secure baseline configurations, automate security hardening during deployment, and regularly audit deployed systems against security benchmarks like CIS Benchmarks.

name: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key (IDOR)
description: Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) occurs when software uses user-controllable input (such as incrementing IDs, filenames, or GUIDs) to directly access internal objects without verifying that the requesting user is authorized to access the specific object. Attackers enumerate or manipulate these references to access other users records, files, or functionality. For example changing user_id=123 to user_id=124 in an API request to access another users profile, or modifying an order reference to view another customers order details. IDOR is one of the most common and exploitable authorization vulnerabilities because it requires no special tools or expertise. Prevention requires implementing server-side authorization checks that verify the requesting user has permission to access the specific requested object, using indirect references that map to internal objects, and avoiding predictable identifiers.

name: Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)
description: CSRF occurs when a web application does not verify that a request was intentionally sent by the authenticated user and not triggered by a malicious site visited by the user. Attackers create pages or emails containing hidden requests (via image tags, JavaScript, or auto-submitting forms) that execute actions on vulnerable sites using the victims authenticated session. Common attack scenarios include changing account email addresses, initiating money transfers, modifying security settings, creating admin accounts, and any state-changing operation. The SameSite cookie attribute, anti-CSRF tokens (synchronizer token pattern), double-submit cookies, and requiring re-authentication for sensitive operations provide defense. Modern frameworks like Django, Rails, and Spring Security include built-in CSRF protection, but developers must ensure it is properly configured and not disabled.

name: URL Redirection to Untrusted Site (Open Redirect)
description: Open redirect occurs when software accepts a user-controlled URL parameter and uses it to redirect the user to that URL without adequate validation. Attackers craft URLs that redirect through the trusted domain to a malicious site, leveraging the trust users place in the legitimate domain for phishing attacks, credential harvesting, or malware distribution. An open redirect on a banking domain makes phishing significantly more effective because the initial URL appears legitimate. Open redirects also facilitate OAuth token theft by redirecting authorization codes to attacker-controlled endpoints. While often considered low severity in isolation, open redirects are powerful components in attack chains combining phishing, OAuth abuse, and SSRF. Prevention requires validating redirect URLs against a strict allowlist of permitted domains and paths.

name: Externally Controlled Reference to a Resource in Another Sphere
description: This weakness occurs when software uses an externally-provided reference (URL, file path, service identifier) to access a resource in a different trust sphere without adequate validation. This encompasses SSRF (CWE-918), open redirects (CWE-601), and file inclusion vulnerabilities where the attacker controls which external resource is accessed. The fundamental issue is trusting external input to specify cross-boundary resource access, which can be exploited to access internal networks, cloud metadata services, local files, or other protected resources that the attacker cannot directly reach. Defense requires strict validation of all external references, implementing allowlists for permitted targets, and using network-level controls (egress filtering, internal firewalls) as defense in depth.

name: Improper Restriction of Rendered UI Layers or Frames (Clickjacking)
description: Clickjacking occurs when software does not restrict how its UI can be rendered within frames controlled by other sites, allowing attackers to overlay transparent or opaque layers that trick users into clicking on hidden elements. Attackers create malicious pages that frame the target application and position invisible buttons or links over seemingly innocent UI elements, causing users to unknowingly perform actions like changing security settings, initiating transactions, or granting permissions. Techniques include transparent iframe overlays, drag-and-drop cross-frame interactions, and cursorjacking. Prevention requires setting X-Frame-Options headers (DENY or SAMEORIGIN), implementing Content-Security-Policy frame-ancestors directive, and using JavaScript frame-busting as defense in depth. CSP frame-ancestors is the modern and more flexible approach.

name: Insufficient Verification of Data Authenticity
description: This weakness occurs when software does not sufficiently verify the authenticity or origin of data it receives and processes. Without proper verification, attackers can forge, tamper with, or replay data that the application trusts. This applies to unsigned software updates (allowing man-in-the-middle injection of malware), API responses without HMAC or signature verification, inter-service communication without mutual authentication, webhook payloads without signature validation, and configuration data loaded from external sources. The impact depends on what the unverified data controls but frequently includes code execution (via injected updates), data corruption, authentication bypass, and business logic manipulation. Prevention requires cryptographic verification (digital signatures, HMAC, checksums) of all data received from external or semi-trusted sources.

name: Origin Validation Error
description: This weakness occurs when software does not properly verify the origin of data or communication, allowing untrusted sources to send requests or data that is processed as if it came from a trusted source. Common manifestations include missing CORS origin validation, accepting API calls from any origin, not validating webhook source IP addresses, trusting X-Forwarded-For headers from untrusted proxies, and missing referrer checks. Attackers exploit origin validation errors to perform cross-origin attacks, forge API requests, impersonate trusted services, and bypass access controls that depend on request origin. Prevention requires strict origin validation using cryptographic methods (signed tokens, mutual TLS) rather than easily spoofable attributes (IP addresses, referrer headers, user-agent strings) where possible.

name: Improper Verification of Cryptographic Signature
description: This weakness occurs when software does not properly verify digital signatures or message authentication codes before processing the signed data. This includes accepting data without checking the signature at all, using weak signature verification that can be bypassed (algorithm confusion attacks in JWT where 'none' algorithm is accepted), not validating the signing certificate chain, or verifying against the wrong public key. JWT-specific attacks include algorithm switching (RS256 to HS256 using the public key as HMAC secret), null signature acceptance, and JWK header injection. Successful exploitation allows attackers to forge authenticated messages, bypass integrity checks on software updates, impersonate trusted parties, and tamper with signed data. All signed data must have its signature verified before processing, using the correct algorithm and key material.

name: Concurrent Execution using Shared Resource with Improper Synchronization (Race Condition)
description: Race conditions occur when software accesses shared resources in a way that depends on the sequence or timing of events, and proper synchronization is not implemented to ensure exclusive access. In security contexts, race conditions most commonly manifest as Time-of-Check to Time-of-Use (TOCTOU) vulnerabilities where a security check is performed at one point but the checked resource changes before it is used. Attackers exploit race conditions by sending concurrent requests to trigger the vulnerable window, potentially achieving privilege escalation, authentication bypass, double-spending in financial systems, and file system manipulation. The vulnerability window may be very small but can be reliably exploited using techniques like request racing/batching and symlink attacks. Prevention requires atomic operations, proper locking mechanisms, and avoiding multi-step operations where security checks and actions are separated.

name: Time-of-check Time-of-use (TOCTOU) Race Condition
description: TOCTOU is a specific race condition where software checks the state of a resource, then uses that resource based on the check, but the resource state can change between the check and the use. Classic examples include checking file permissions then opening the file (attacker swaps the file via symlink between check and open), verifying account balance then debiting (attacker submits concurrent transaction between check and debit), and checking user permissions then performing action (permissions revoked between check and action). File system TOCTOU vulnerabilities are particularly common in privileged programs that check file ownership or permissions before performing operations. Prevention requires using atomic operations that combine the check and use into a single operation, using file descriptors rather than file names, and implementing proper locking.

name: Uncontrolled Resource Consumption
description: This weakness occurs when software does not properly control the amount of resources (CPU, memory, disk space, network bandwidth, file handles, database connections) that can be consumed by an actor, enabling denial of service. Attackers exploit this by sending requests that trigger expensive operations (complex regex matching, large file processing, recursive algorithms), uploading excessively large files, creating many concurrent connections, or triggering excessive logging. Specific patterns include ReDoS (Regular Expression Denial of Service), ZIP bomb attacks, XML entity expansion (Billion Laughs), hash collision attacks against hash table implementations, and algorithmic complexity attacks. Prevention requires setting resource limits at multiple layers: request size limits, timeout constraints, rate limiting, connection pooling, and monitoring resource consumption with automatic throttling.

name: Allocation of Resources Without Limits or Throttling
description: This weakness occurs when software allocates resources such as memory, CPU cycles, file handles, network connections, or threads without imposing any limit on the quantity that can be allocated to a single actor or in total. Without limits, a single malicious or misbehaving client can exhaust all available resources, causing denial of service for legitimate users. Common examples include unlimited file upload sizes, unbounded query result sets, unlimited concurrent WebSocket connections per user, no rate limiting on API endpoints, and unlimited thread or process creation per request. Prevention requires implementing resource quotas (per-user, per-IP, per-session), API rate limiting, pagination for data queries, maximum request size limits, connection pooling with upper bounds, and circuit breakers for cascading failure prevention.

name: Missing Release of Resource after Effective Lifetime
description: This weakness occurs when software does not release allocated resources (memory, file handles, database connections, locks, sockets) after they are no longer needed, leading to resource leaks that accumulate over time. In long-running server applications, persistent resource leaks eventually exhaust available resources, causing degraded performance and denial of service. Attackers can accelerate resource exhaustion by triggering code paths that leak resources, such as error handling paths that fail to clean up, timeout scenarios that skip resource release, and exceptional conditions that bypass finally blocks. Prevention requires deterministic resource management (using with/using/try-with-resources patterns), connection pooling with lifecycle management, resource monitoring and alerting, and thorough testing of error handling paths for proper cleanup.

name: Improper Resource Shutdown or Release
description: This weakness occurs when software does not properly release or shut down resources when they are no longer needed or when cleanup should occur. This includes not closing file handles, database connections, network sockets, or releasing locks during shutdown or error conditions. Unlike CWE-772 which focuses on lifetime management, this weakness also covers improper shutdown sequences that leave resources in inconsistent states, partial cleanup that releases some but not all resources, and failure to clean up sensitive data from memory before release. Improper resource management can lead to resource exhaustion, data corruption, information leakage from reused resources (uncleared buffers), and deadlocks from unreleased locks.

name: Improper Neutralization of Formula Elements in a CSV File
description: CSV injection (also called formula injection) occurs when software incorporates user-controlled input into CSV files without neutralizing formula elements (cells starting with =, +, -, @, tab, or carriage return). When the CSV is opened in spreadsheet applications like Excel or Google Sheets, these formulas execute and can call external commands (=CMD|), access local files, exfiltrate data via hyperlinks, or make external network requests that leak data. While the exploitation requires the victim to open the CSV in a spreadsheet application and potentially dismiss security warnings, it remains a practical social engineering vector, particularly for data export features in business applications. Prevention requires prefixing suspicious cell values with a single quote or tab character to prevent formula interpretation.

name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements in Data Query Logic (NoSQL Injection)
description: NoSQL injection occurs when software constructs NoSQL database queries (MongoDB, CouchDB, Redis, Elasticsearch) using externally-influenced input without proper neutralization. Unlike SQL injection, NoSQL injection exploits the query syntax of specific database systems, often using JSON or JavaScript operators. In MongoDB, attackers use operators like $gt, $ne, $regex in query parameters to bypass authentication or extract data. NoSQL injection can also involve JavaScript injection in databases that support server-side JavaScript execution (MongoDB $where clauses). The impact is similar to SQL injection: authentication bypass, unauthorized data access, data modification, and in some cases code execution. Prevention requires using parameterized queries, input type validation, disabling server-side JavaScript where possible, and applying the principle of least privilege to database accounts.

name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an LDAP Query (LDAP Injection)
description: LDAP injection occurs when software constructs LDAP queries using externally-influenced input without neutralizing special characters like *, (, ), \, NUL, and the boolean operators & and |. Attackers craft input that alters LDAP query logic to bypass authentication, enumerate directory information, access unauthorized LDAP entries, or modify directory data. For example, injecting *)(&) into a username field can alter a query to match all users, bypassing authentication. In environments where LDAP controls access to enterprise resources (Active Directory, OpenLDAP), LDAP injection can lead to unauthorized access to the entire corporate directory, user credential exposure, and privilege escalation. Prevention requires parameterized LDAP queries, strict input validation against LDAP special characters, and least-privilege LDAP bind accounts.

name: XML Injection (aka Blind XPath Injection)
description: XML/XPath injection occurs when software uses externally-influenced input in XPath expressions or XML queries without proper neutralization. Attackers inject XPath syntax to modify query logic, extract data from XML documents, or bypass authentication based on XML data stores. Blind XPath injection uses boolean-based or time-based inference techniques to extract data character by character when results are not directly visible. Unlike SQL injection, XPath has no equivalent of UNION SELECT, but the entire XML document can be extracted through iterative XPath queries using position() and string functions. This is particularly relevant in applications using XML-based data storage, SOAP web services, SAML authentication, and configuration files queried via XPath.

name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements Used in a Template Engine (SSTI)
description: Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) occurs when user input is embedded directly into template engine expressions rather than being passed as data to the template. Template engines like Jinja2 (Python), Twig (PHP), Freemarker (Java), Velocity (Java), ERB (Ruby), and Pebble (Java) provide powerful expression languages that can access the full programming language runtime. Attackers inject template syntax that escapes the data context and executes arbitrary code on the server. SSTI is often initially dismissed as XSS but is far more severe because execution occurs server-side. Detection involves injecting mathematical expressions ({{7*7}}) and checking if the template engine evaluates them. Prevention requires never embedding user input in template strings and always using template engine data binding.

name: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements in Output Used by a Downstream Component (Injection)
description: This is the parent category for all injection vulnerabilities where software generates output that is sent to a downstream component (SQL database, OS shell, XML parser, LDAP server, template engine, mail server, or web browser) without neutralizing special elements that have significance to that component. The downstream component interprets the injected elements as commands or control instructions rather than data, allowing attackers to alter the intended behavior. This encompasses SQL injection, OS command injection, XSS, LDAP injection, XML injection, header injection, log injection, and many other injection variants. The universal prevention principle is to use parameterized interfaces that separate data from control structures, and when not available, to apply context-appropriate encoding or escaping.

name: Improper Encoding or Escaping of Output
description: This weakness occurs when software prepares output for a downstream component but does not properly encode or escape the output for the specific context in which it will be interpreted. Even when input validation is performed, output encoding is necessary because the same data may be safe in one context but dangerous in another. For example, data safe in HTML body context needs different encoding for HTML attributes, JavaScript strings, CSS values, and URL parameters. Common manifestations include missing HTML entity encoding leading to XSS, missing URL encoding leading to parameter pollution, missing shell escaping leading to command injection, and missing SQL escaping when parameterized queries are not available. Context-aware output encoding is the primary defense against injection attacks.

name: Inappropriate Encoding for Output
description: This weakness is a variant of CWE-116 where the software applies encoding or escaping but uses the wrong encoding scheme for the output context. For example, applying HTML entity encoding to data placed in a JavaScript context does not prevent XSS because the JavaScript interpreter processes the data before the HTML parser. Similarly, URL encoding data placed in HTML attributes, or HTML encoding data used in LDAP queries, provides no protection. The correct encoding must match the interpretation context of the downstream component. Web applications are particularly susceptible because a single page may contain multiple encoding contexts (HTML, JavaScript, CSS, URL) requiring different encoding strategies for each.

name: Use of Externally-Controlled Format String
description: Format string vulnerabilities occur when software uses externally-influenced input as a format string parameter in functions like printf(), sprintf(), syslog(), or their equivalents. In C/C++, format specifiers like %s, %x, and %n allow attackers to read from the stack (%x reveals memory contents), write to arbitrary memory locations (%n writes the number of bytes printed so far), and crash the application. While primarily a C/C++ issue, format string attacks can also affect logging frameworks in other languages that interpret format specifiers in user-supplied data. Exploitation can achieve information disclosure, denial of service, and arbitrary code execution. Prevention requires never using user input as format strings, always using fixed format strings with user data as arguments, and using compiler warnings (-Wformat-security) to detect potential issues.

name: Untrusted Search Path
description: This weakness occurs when software uses a search path to locate critical resources such as libraries, executables, or configuration files, and the search path includes directories that are under attacker control. The most common manifestation is DLL search order hijacking on Windows where an attacker places a malicious DLL in a directory that is searched before the legitimate DLL location (such as the application working directory). Similarly on Linux, manipulating LD_LIBRARY_PATH, PATH, or PYTHONPATH can redirect library or executable loading. This enables code execution with the privileges of the vulnerable application, which is particularly dangerous for privileged services and SUID/SGID programs. Prevention requires using fully-qualified paths, restricting search paths, validating loaded resources, and setting secure directory permissions.

name: Uncontrolled Search Path Element
description: This weakness occurs when software uses a search path that includes an element controlled by an attacker, typically through environment variable manipulation, writable directories in the system PATH, or current directory inclusion in search paths. On Windows, the default DLL search order includes the application directory and current working directory before system directories, enabling DLL planting attacks. On Linux, writable directories in LD_LIBRARY_PATH or executables with RPATH pointing to writable locations enable similar attacks. This is commonly exploited in privilege escalation scenarios where a low-privilege attacker places malicious libraries in locations searched by privileged applications. The distinction from CWE-426 is that this weakness focuses on uncontrolled elements within the search path rather than the untrusted path as a whole.

name: Unquoted Search Path or Element
description: This weakness occurs on Windows when software specifies a path to an executable or service that contains spaces but is not enclosed in quotation marks. Windows resolves unquoted paths by trying each space-delimited segment as a potential executable. For a service configured with ImagePath = C:\Program Files\My App\service.exe, Windows will try C:\Program.exe first, then C:\Program Files\My.exe, before finally trying the intended path. Attackers who can create files at these intermediate paths achieve code execution with the privileges of the service (often SYSTEM). This is a common privilege escalation vector on Windows systems and is readily discovered by tools like PowerUp and winPEAS. Prevention requires quoting all paths containing spaces in service configurations, scheduled tasks, and registry entries.

name: Download of Code Without Integrity Check
description: This weakness occurs when software downloads code (updates, plugins, libraries, scripts) from a remote source without verifying its integrity through cryptographic signatures, hashes, or other integrity verification mechanisms. Attackers who can intercept or modify the download (through man-in-the-middle attacks, DNS hijacking, compromised mirrors, or CDN compromise) can replace legitimate code with malicious versions. This is the technical weakness behind many software supply chain attacks. Notable examples include the CCleaner supply chain compromise, SolarWinds Orion backdoor, and various npm/PyPI package hijacking incidents. Prevention requires verifying digital signatures from trusted publishers, checking cryptographic hashes obtained through separate secure channels, using pinned hashes for dependencies (package-lock.json, Pipfile.lock), and implementing transparency logs (Sigstore, TUF).

name: Inclusion of Functionality from Untrusted Control Sphere
description: This weakness occurs when software includes or imports functionality from a source outside its trust boundary without adequate verification. This encompasses loading JavaScript from third-party CDNs without Subresource Integrity (SRI), importing packages that could be replaced through dependency confusion attacks, including remote files (PHP include of remote URLs), referencing external scripts or stylesheets that could be compromised, and using third-party APIs or services without verifying response integrity. Attackers who compromise the external source gain code execution within the trusting application. The scope and supply chain implications make this particularly dangerous in modern application development where applications commonly depend on hundreds of external packages and services.

name: Embedded Malicious Code
description: This weakness describes software that contains code specifically designed to perform unauthorized functions, acting as a backdoor, trojan, logic bomb, or time bomb. Unlike vulnerabilities that arise from programming errors, embedded malicious code is intentionally placed by an insider threat, compromised development environment, or supply chain attacker. Examples include hidden admin accounts in firmware, time-delayed data exfiltration, conditional logic bombs triggered by specific dates or events, and cryptocurrency miners embedded in popular packages. Detection is challenging because the malicious code may be obfuscated, dormant for extended periods, or disguised as legitimate functionality. Prevention relies on code review, behavioral analysis, supply chain security controls, and monitoring for anomalous behavior in deployed software.

name: Use of Unmaintained Third-Party Components
description: This weakness occurs when software depends on third-party components (libraries, frameworks, services) that are no longer actively maintained, meaning security vulnerabilities discovered after abandonment will never be patched. Unmaintained dependencies accumulate vulnerabilities over time and become increasingly dangerous as disclosed CVEs are never fixed. The risk is amplified when the unmaintained component handles security-sensitive operations like authentication, encryption, or input parsing. Identifying unmaintained dependencies requires monitoring project activity (commit frequency, issue response time, last release date) and establishing organizational policies for evaluating and replacing abandoned dependencies. Software Composition Analysis (SCA) tools can automate detection of outdated and unmaintained components.

name: Use of Components with Known Vulnerabilities
description: This weakness occurs when software incorporates third-party components (libraries, frameworks, operating system packages) with publicly known and documented security vulnerabilities. Using vulnerable components is one of the most common application security weaknesses because modern applications depend on hundreds of direct and transitive dependencies, any of which may have known CVEs. Attackers use tools like Vulners, NVD, and Snyk to find applications using vulnerable component versions and apply known exploits with minimal effort. Notable exploitation includes Equifax breach (Apache Struts CVE-2017-5638), Log4Shell (Log4j CVE-2021-44228), and Spring4Shell (Spring Framework CVE-2022-22965). Prevention requires automated dependency scanning in CI/CD, keeping components updated, monitoring security advisories, and maintaining a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM).

name: Use of Potentially Dangerous Function
description: This weakness occurs when software uses functions that are known to be inherently dangerous due to their inability to perform bounds checking, their susceptibility to format string attacks, or other inherent design limitations. In C/C++, this includes strcpy(), strcat(), sprintf(), gets(), scanf() without width specifiers, and system(). These functions have safer alternatives: strncpy()/strlcpy(), strncat()/strlcat(), snprintf(), fgets(), and parameterized execution APIs. Many compilers and static analysis tools flag usage of these dangerous functions, and some organizations (including Microsoft through its Security Development Lifecycle) ban their use entirely. While not all uses of dangerous functions are exploitable, replacing them with safe alternatives eliminates entire categories of potential vulnerabilities.

name: Exposure of Resource to Wrong Sphere
description: This weakness occurs when software makes a resource available to actors in a security sphere beyond the one intended by the developer. This is a broad category encompassing files accessible to wrong users, internal services exposed to the internet, sensitive API endpoints accessible cross-origin, database ports exposed to public networks, and internal configuration exposed through public interfaces. The concept of security spheres distinguishes between intended access boundaries: internal network vs internet, user space vs kernel space, tenant A vs tenant B in multi-tenant systems. Attackers who discover resources exposed to the wrong sphere often gain unexpected access without needing to exploit any traditional vulnerability. Prevention requires explicit access control at every boundary and regular verification that resources are only accessible from intended spheres.

name: Use of Incorrectly-Resolved Name or Reference
description: This weakness occurs when software uses a name or reference to access a resource but the name resolves to an unintended resource, either due to environmental manipulation, name collision, or ambiguous resolution rules. Examples include DNS rebinding attacks where a hostname resolves to different IPs at different times, file path canonicalization bypass where symlinks or alternative path representations (8.3 filenames, UNC paths, URL encoding) resolve differently than expected, and package namespace confusion where a public package shadows an internal one. This is the underlying weakness behind dependency confusion attacks where attackers publish public packages with the same name as internal packages, which are then installed instead of the intended internal version. Prevention requires canonicalizing names and references before use, implementing proper namespace isolation, and using immutable references where possible.

name: Sensitive Cookie with Improper SameSite Attribute
description: This weakness occurs when a security-sensitive cookie does not have a proper SameSite attribute, potentially enabling cross-site request forgery (CSRF) attacks. The SameSite attribute controls whether cookies are sent with cross-site requests: Strict prevents the cookie from being sent on any cross-site request; Lax (the current browser default) sends the cookie only with top-level navigations using safe HTTP methods; None sends the cookie with all cross-site requests (requires the Secure attribute). Authentication cookies and session identifiers should use SameSite=Strict or SameSite=Lax to prevent CSRF. Setting SameSite=None is necessary for legitimate cross-site cookie usage (embedded content, OAuth flows) but requires additional CSRF protections.

name: Sensitive Cookie in HTTPS Session Without Secure Attribute
description: This weakness occurs when a cookie containing sensitive information (session identifier, authentication token, CSRF token) is set without the Secure flag, meaning the browser will transmit it over unencrypted HTTP connections in addition to HTTPS. If the user visits any page on the same domain over HTTP (even through a redirect, mixed content, or an attacker-forced downgrade), the sensitive cookie is transmitted in cleartext and can be captured by any network attacker. This enables session hijacking through passive network sniffing. Even when the application exclusively serves content over HTTPS, the Secure flag is necessary because users may type the domain without https:// or follow HTTP links. All sensitive cookies must include the Secure attribute.

name: Sensitive Cookie Without HttpOnly Flag
description: This weakness occurs when a cookie containing sensitive information such as a session identifier does not have the HttpOnly flag set, making it accessible to client-side JavaScript through the document.cookie API. This enables XSS attacks to steal session cookies by injecting scripts that read and exfiltrate cookie values to attacker-controlled servers. The HttpOnly flag provides defense-in-depth against cookie theft via XSS, though it should not be considered a substitute for XSS prevention. Session cookies, authentication tokens, and CSRF tokens should always have the HttpOnly flag set. Cookies that need to be accessible to JavaScript for legitimate application functionality (such as user preference cookies) should not contain sensitive authentication data.

name: Permissive Cross-domain Policy with Untrusted Domains
description: This weakness occurs when software uses overly permissive cross-domain policies (CORS, crossdomain.xml, clientaccesspolicy.xml) that allow untrusted or overly broad origins to access resources. Setting Access-Control-Allow-Origin to * (wildcard) or dynamically reflecting the Origin header without validation allows any website to make authenticated cross-origin requests and read responses. This can expose sensitive API data, enable CSRF-like attacks that read responses, and allow third-party sites to impersonate authenticated users. Combined with credentials (Access-Control-Allow-Credentials: true), permissive CORS policies can lead to complete account takeover. Proper CORS configuration requires an explicit allowlist of trusted origins, avoiding credential sharing with wildcard origins, and minimizing exposed headers and methods.

name: Selection of Less-Secure Algorithm During Negotiation
description: This weakness occurs when software allows protocol negotiation or algorithm selection to choose a weaker algorithm than necessary, enabling downgrade attacks. Attackers who can influence the negotiation process force the use of weak or broken algorithms that they can then exploit. Notable examples include SSL/TLS downgrade attacks (POODLE forcing SSL 3.0, DROWN exploiting SSLv2), SSH algorithm downgrade attacks, and WiFi protocol downgrades (WPA3 to WPA2).
