Prevention requires disabling support for weak algorithms and protocol versions, implementing negotiation integrity checks (TLS 1.3 encrypted handshake), and configuring minimum acceptable security levels. Organizations should maintain updated cipher suite configurations that remove algorithms as they become weak.

name: Missing Encryption of Sensitive Data
description: This weakness occurs when software fails to encrypt sensitive data that requires confidentiality protection, either at rest or in transit. Unencrypted sensitive data is directly accessible to any actor who gains access to the storage medium or communication channel. This includes database columns containing PII stored without column-level encryption, backup files containing unencrypted sensitive data, internal API communications carrying authentication tokens over plaintext, and removable media containing unencrypted sensitive records. The impact of missing encryption is amplified in regulated industries where data protection requirements (GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS) mandate encryption of specific data categories. Prevention requires a data classification scheme that identifies sensitive data, encryption standards for each classification level, and technical controls to enforce encryption requirements.

name: Insecure Storage of Sensitive Information
description: This weakness occurs when software stores sensitive information without adequate safeguards to prevent unauthorized access. This encompasses storing data in world-readable locations, using reversible encryption instead of one-way hashing for passwords, storing cryptographic keys alongside the data they protect, caching sensitive data in browser storage (localStorage, sessionStorage), and storing sensitive data in unprotected mobile device locations. The weakness is broader than cleartext storage (CWE-312) as it includes scenarios where some protection exists but is insufficient, such as using XOR obfuscation, hardcoded encryption keys, or storing encrypted data with the decryption key in the same location. Prevention requires threat-modeling storage locations, applying encryption with properly managed keys, and minimizing the sensitive data that needs to be stored.

name: Insertion of Sensitive Information into Externally-Accessible File or Directory
description: This weakness occurs when software places sensitive information into files or directories that are accessible to actors beyond the intended audience. Common examples include writing sensitive data to files in the web root directory (accessible via HTTP), creating temporary files containing credentials in world-readable directories, storing debug logs with sensitive data in publicly accessible locations, and committing secrets to version control repositories. Combined with directory indexing or path traversal vulnerabilities, this can lead to large-scale data exposure. The Git repository exposure pattern (.git directory accessible via web) has led to many source code leaks containing hardcoded credentials. Prevention requires identifying all locations where data is written and ensuring appropriate access controls follow the data.

name: Improper Output Neutralization for Logs
description: Log injection occurs when software writes user-controllable data to log files without neutralizing characters that have special meaning in the log context. Attackers inject newline characters to forge log entries, potentially covering their tracks by injecting misleading log lines, triggering log-processing systems (SIEM rules, automated parsers) with crafted entries, or exploiting log viewer vulnerabilities (XSS in web-based log viewers, command injection in log processing pipelines). Log4Shell (CVE-2021-44228) demonstrated the extreme danger of log injection when the logging framework itself interpreted log content as commands (JNDI lookups). Prevention requires neutralizing newline and control characters in log output, using structured logging formats (JSON), and ensuring log processing pipelines treat log content as untrusted data.

name: Insufficient Logging
description: This weakness occurs when software does not log security-relevant events or does not include sufficient detail in log entries to support security monitoring, incident response, and forensic analysis. Events that should be logged include authentication successes and failures, authorization failures, input validation failures, application errors, administrative actions, data access and modification, and security configuration changes. Insufficient logging enables attackers to operate undetected for extended periods, drastically increasing the impact of breaches. The median dwell time (time between compromise and detection) for organizations with poor logging is measured in months. Effective security logging requires defining a comprehensive logging policy, implementing centralized log aggregation, correlating events across systems, and establishing alerting thresholds for suspicious patterns.

name: Omission of Security-relevant Information
description: This weakness occurs when software does not record or display information that is important for identifying security-relevant events or making security decisions. This includes failing to log the source IP address of requests, omitting user identity from audit trails, not recording the specific permission that was denied in authorization failures, and not including timestamps or correlation IDs that would enable event reconstruction. Without this information, security teams cannot effectively investigate incidents, detect attack patterns, or satisfy compliance requirements for audit trails. This is the qualitative complement to CWE-778 (insufficient logging); even when events are logged, missing security-relevant details can render the logs useless for security purposes.

name: Improper Check for Unusual or Exceptional Conditions
description: This weakness occurs when software does not properly check for conditions that are unusual or unexpected but could indicate security-related issues. Examples include not checking return values from security-critical functions (authentication, authorization, cryptographic operations), not handling unusual input combinations that bypass business logic, ignoring error codes from system calls, and not detecting anomalous system states. Attackers often exploit edge cases and exceptional conditions to bypass security controls, counting on the software to fail open rather than fail securely when confronted with unexpected situations. Defensive programming requires checking all return values, handling all error conditions, implementing fail-secure defaults, and testing with boundary conditions, null values, and maximum-length inputs.

name: Improper Handling of Exceptional Conditions
description: This weakness occurs when software detects an exceptional condition but does not handle it properly, leading to security consequences. This differs from CWE-754 in that the condition is detected but the response is inappropriate. Examples include catching all exceptions with empty catch blocks (swallowing errors), logging the error but continuing with default values that bypass security checks, displaying detailed error information to users, and failing to clean up sensitive data from memory during error handling. Poor exception handling can lead to information disclosure, denial of service, bypassed security checks, and inconsistent application state that creates further vulnerabilities. Exception handling should follow the principle of failing securely: denying access, cleaning up resources, and logging details server-side while showing generic errors to users.

name: Error Handling Issues
description: This is a parent category for weaknesses related to improper handling of errors and exceptional conditions. It encompasses missing error handling (CWE-754), incorrect error handling (CWE-755), information exposure through error messages (CWE-209), and uncaught exceptions that cause application crashes. Error handling is a critical security concern because it determines how software behaves when its assumptions are violated, which is precisely what attackers attempt to achieve. Robust error handling requires a consistent strategy across the application: security-relevant events are logged with detail, users receive generic error messages, resources are properly cleaned up, and the application transitions to a secure state. Many security vulnerabilities are only exploitable because error handling paths have not been thoroughly tested or reviewed.

name: Inefficient Regular Expression Complexity (ReDoS)
description: Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS) occurs when software uses regular expressions that are vulnerable to catastrophic backtracking when evaluated against specially crafted input strings. Regex engines using backtracking (NFA-based, used by most programming languages including Python, Java, JavaScript, .NET, and Ruby) can exhibit exponential time complexity with patterns containing nested quantifiers, overlapping alternations, or ambiguous repetitions. For example, the pattern (a+)+ against the input aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaX causes the regex engine to explore 2^n matching paths before failing. Attackers submit input strings designed to trigger worst-case regex evaluation, consuming CPU resources and causing application hangs or denial of service. Prevention requires using regex engines with linear-time guarantees (RE2, Rust regex), setting evaluation timeouts, avoiding vulnerable pattern constructs, and testing regex patterns with ReDoS detection tools.

name: Improperly Controlled Modification of Object Prototype Attributes (Prototype Pollution)
description: Prototype pollution occurs in JavaScript/Node.js applications when an attacker can modify the prototype of base objects (Object.prototype) through operations that recursively merge, clone, or set object properties using user-controlled keys. By setting properties on Object.prototype through paths like __proto__.isAdmin or constructor.prototype.role, attackers inject properties that are inherited by all objects in the application, potentially bypassing security checks, enabling unauthorized access, or achieving remote code execution. Prototype pollution through JSON.parse with recursive merge functions is common in Node.js applications using libraries like lodash.merge, jQuery.extend, and similar deep-merge utilities. Exploitation can lead to XSS, authentication bypass, RCE (via poisoning child_process options or template engine settings), and denial of service. Prevention requires freezing Object.prototype, using Map instead of plain objects, and validating that user-controlled keys do not include __proto__, constructor, or prototype.

name: Improperly Controlled Modification of Dynamically-Determined Object Attributes (Mass Assignment)
description: Mass assignment (also called auto-binding or object injection) occurs when software automatically binds user-supplied input to internal object properties without restricting which properties can be set. Web frameworks that automatically map request parameters to model attributes (Rails, Django, Spring, Express with body-parser) are particularly susceptible. Attackers include unexpected parameters in requests to modify properties like is_admin, role, price, verified, or account_balance that should only be set by the application internally. For example, sending role=admin in a user registration form to create an administrator account. Prevention requires explicit allowlists of bindable properties (strong parameters in Rails, serializer fields in Django REST Framework), using separate DTOs for input that map only permitted fields, and never directly binding request data to database models or security-sensitive objects.

name: Multiple Releases of Same Resource or Handle
description: This weakness occurs when software releases a resource or handle more than once, commonly known as a double-free when applied to memory. Double-free corrupts the memory allocator metadata, potentially allowing an attacker to control subsequent memory allocations and achieve arbitrary code execution. When the same memory region is freed twice and then allocated again, two different pointers may reference the same memory location, creating a use-after-free condition that can be exploited. Double-free vulnerabilities are common in C/C++ applications with complex resource lifecycle management, error handling paths that free resources already freed in the normal path, and concurrent access to shared resources. Modern heap implementations include double-free detection, but these protections can sometimes be bypassed through heap manipulation techniques.

name: Double Free
description: Double free is the specific memory corruption vulnerability where the same memory allocation is freed twice through calls to free(), delete, or equivalent deallocation functions. The second free operation corrupts the heap allocator free list, which attackers can manipulate to achieve an arbitrary write primitive that leads to code execution. Exploitation involves causing the allocator to return the same memory region for two different allocations, allowing controlled overlap of objects. Sophisticated exploitation techniques combine double-free with heap spraying and type confusion to reliably achieve code execution despite modern heap protections (safe unlinking, heap cookies). Double-free vulnerabilities are discovered through fuzzing, address sanitizers (ASan), and static analysis tools. Memory-safe languages eliminate this class of vulnerability entirely.

name: Divide By Zero
description: Divide by zero occurs when software performs a division or modulo operation where the divisor is zero, leading to application crashes, undefined behavior, or infinite values depending on the language and data type. While primarily a reliability issue causing denial of service through application crashes, divide by zero can have security implications in specific contexts: floating-point division by zero producing Infinity or NaN can corrupt calculations used for access control decisions, financial transactions, or resource allocation. In C/C++, integer division by zero triggers undefined behavior which optimizing compilers may exploit in unexpected ways, potentially eliminating security checks. Prevention requires validating that divisors are non-zero before division operations and handling the zero case appropriately.

name: Access of Resource Using Incompatible Type (Type Confusion)
description: Type confusion occurs when software accesses a resource using a type that is not compatible with the actual type of the resource, leading to interpretation of memory contents in unintended ways. In C/C++, this often results from incorrect casting, union misuse, or virtual table corruption. In JavaScript, type confusion in JIT-compiled code exploits incorrect type assumptions to achieve out-of-bounds reads and writes. Type confusion vulnerabilities in browsers and PDF readers are among the most commonly exploited in the wild, frequently appearing in zero-day exploit chains. Exploitation typically gives the attacker the ability to read and write arbitrary memory, leading to reliable code execution. Browser JIT type confusion is a primary exploit primitive for sandbox escape chains used by advanced threat actors and commercial spyware vendors.

name: Incorrect Type Conversion or Cast
description: This weakness occurs when software converts or casts data from one type to another incorrectly, leading to unexpected values, truncation, sign errors, or reinterpretation of data. Common examples include casting a larger integer type to a smaller type causing truncation, converting signed integers to unsigned (negative values becoming large positive values), narrowing conversions in C/C++ that lose data, and implicit type coercion in dynamically-typed languages that produces unexpected results. Security implications arise when the incorrectly converted value is used in size calculations (leading to buffer overflows), comparison operations (leading to bypass of checks), or memory reference operations. Prevention requires explicit validation of conversion target ranges, using safe conversion functions that detect overflow, and avoiding implicit type coercion in security-critical code.

name: Binding to an Unrestricted IP Address
description: This weakness occurs when software binds a network socket to all available interfaces (0.0.0.0 or ::) when it should only be listening on a specific interface such as localhost (127.0.0.1) or an internal network interface. Services intended only for local access (database servers, debug interfaces, management APIs, development servers) that bind to 0.0.0.0 become accessible from the network, potentially exposing them to unauthorized remote access. This is particularly dangerous in cloud environments where instance network interfaces may be directly or indirectly accessible from the internet. Many database breaches (exposed MongoDB, Elasticsearch, Redis instances) have resulted from services binding to all interfaces with default or no authentication. Prevention requires explicitly specifying the minimum necessary bind address and using firewall rules as defense in depth.

name: Configuration
description: This is a broad category for security weaknesses that arise from how software is configured rather than from programming errors in the code itself. Configuration weaknesses include default credentials, unnecessary services enabled, debug mode active in production, verbose error messages, overly permissive access controls, disabled security features, insecure protocol versions, and missing security headers. Configuration weaknesses are among the most common and easily exploitable because they require no code changes to fix, only configuration updates, yet organizations consistently fail to secure configurations across their infrastructure. Automated security configuration scanning (CIS Benchmarks, Docker Bench, kube-bench), infrastructure-as-code with security policies, and configuration drift detection help maintain secure configurations at scale.

name: Insecure Default Initialization of Resource
description: This weakness occurs when software initializes resources (variables, data structures, configurations, services) with default values that are not secure. Examples include uninitialized security flags defaulting to false (granting access), debug mode enabled by default, authentication disabled by default, encryption using weak default algorithms or key sizes, and default accounts with well-known credentials. The principle of secure defaults requires that software is secure out-of-the-box without requiring the user to make configuration changes. Users who do not modify default settings (which is the majority in practice) should still have a secure posture. This weakness is related to security misconfiguration (CWE-16) but specifically addresses the default state established by the software developer or vendor.

name: Weak Authentication
description: This weakness occurs when software uses authentication mechanisms that are inherently weak or easily bypassed by attackers. This includes reliance on easily spoofable attributes (IP address, user-agent, referrer header) for authentication, single-factor authentication for sensitive operations, authentication tokens with insufficient entropy, protocols without replay protection, challenge-response systems with predictable challenges, and custom authentication schemes that have not been cryptographically analyzed. Weak authentication is distinct from missing authentication (CWE-306) in that an authentication mechanism exists but provides insufficient security. Prevention requires using well-established authentication protocols and frameworks, implementing multi-factor authentication for sensitive operations, and avoiding custom authentication designs without expert cryptographic review.

name: Use of Weak Credentials
description: This weakness occurs when software allows or uses credentials that can be easily discovered, guessed, or brute-forced by attackers. This includes allowing empty passwords, common passwords (password, 123456, admin), credentials matching the username, short PINs without lockout, and default credentials that are not required to be changed on first use. Weak credentials are the most common entry point for unauthorized access across all system types: web applications, databases, network devices, IoT devices, and cloud services. Credential breach databases contain billions of password entries. Prevention requires enforcing minimum password strength requirements, checking against breached password databases, requiring MFA, and forcing credential changes on initial setup for default accounts.

name: Files or Directories Accessible to External Parties
description: This weakness occurs when software makes files or directories accessible to parties outside the intended access boundary. Common examples include exposing .git directories, backup files (.bak, .old, .swp), configuration files (web.config, .env, wp-config.php), database files, source code, and internal documentation through web servers. Automated scanning tools routinely check for these exposures, and search engines index accidentally exposed files. The impact ranges from information disclosure (source code, credentials) to complete system compromise when configuration files contain database passwords or API keys. Prevention requires configuring web servers to deny access to sensitive file types and directories, placing sensitive files outside the web root, regular security scans for exposed files, and implementing proper .gitignore and deployment exclusion rules.

name: Improper Preservation of Permissions
description: This weakness occurs when software does not properly maintain the security permissions of resources when they are copied, moved, backed up, restored, or otherwise transferred. File copy operations may not preserve ACLs, creating files with default (potentially more permissive) permissions. Archive extraction may create files with permissions from the archive rather than the current security policy. Database migrations may not preserve row-level security policies. Container image layer operations may modify file permissions unexpectedly. This can silently widen access to sensitive resources without any direct action by an attacker, creating opportunities for unauthorized access discovered later through routine enumeration.

name: Incorrect Privilege Assignment
description: This weakness occurs when software assigns incorrect privileges to a particular actor, granting more access than intended. This includes creating user accounts with admin privileges by default, assigning overly broad database roles, granting cloud IAM policies with admin access for convenience, and privilege inheritance that grants child objects broader access than intended. Incorrect privilege assignment violates the principle of least privilege and creates opportunities for both accidental misuse and deliberate exploitation. Once an account with excessive privileges is compromised, the attacker gains access to all resources those privileges protect. Prevention requires defining minimum required privileges for each role, implementing privilege request and approval workflows, and conducting regular privilege audits to identify and remediate over-privileged accounts.

name: Improper Control of Dynamically-Managed Code Resources
description: This weakness occurs when software does not properly restrict the creation, modification, or execution of dynamically managed code resources. This includes allowing untrusted code to be loaded and executed (eval, dynamic imports, plugin systems without sandboxing), permitting modification of executable code at runtime, and failing to validate dynamically generated code before execution. Server-side template injection, dynamic code compilation with user input, and unrestricted plugin/extension loading are common manifestations. The impact is typically remote code execution since the attacker can introduce arbitrary executable code into the application. Prevention requires avoiding dynamic code generation from user input, implementing strict sandboxing for plugin systems, using CSP to control client-side script execution, and applying code signing for dynamically loaded components.

name: Modification of Assumed-Immutable Data (MAID)
description: This weakness occurs when software does not properly protect data that it assumes cannot be modified by an attacker. Developers often assume certain data is immutable because it is stored in hidden form fields, cookies, headers, or other client-side locations, but attackers can modify any data under their control. Similarly, data in shared memory, temporary files, or inter-process communication channels may be assumed immutable but is modifiable by other processes. Common examples include trusting hidden form field values for pricing or authorization, relying on client-side JavaScript validation as the sole security control, and trusting data from earlier pipeline stages without re-validation. Prevention requires treating all data from outside the trust boundary as potentially tampered and implementing server-side validation and enforcement for all security-relevant decisions.

name: SQL Injection: Hibernate
description: This is a specific variant of SQL injection that occurs in applications using Hibernate ORM when developers construct HQL (Hibernate Query Language) or Criteria queries using string concatenation with user input rather than using parameterized queries. While Hibernate provides parameterized query support through named parameters and the Criteria API, developers sometimes bypass these safe methods by building HQL strings directly. HQL injection allows attackers to manipulate queries to access unauthorized data, modify records, or bypass authentication. The exploitation is similar to traditional SQL injection but uses HQL syntax. Prevention requires consistently using Hibernate named parameters (:paramName), Criteria API predicates, or JPA specification predicates instead of string concatenation in all query construction.

name: Improper Neutralization of Directives in Dynamically Evaluated Code (Eval Injection)
description: Eval injection occurs when software uses functions like eval(), exec(), Function(), or equivalent dynamic code evaluation mechanisms with input that contains user-controllable data. The dynamic evaluation mechanism interprets the input as code and executes it within the application context, giving attackers the ability to execute arbitrary code on the server or client. Common vulnerable patterns include eval() of JSON (use JSON.parse instead), eval() of mathematical expressions, dynamic method lookup using user input, and template systems that call eval. In Python, eval/exec with user input enables os.system() calls; in JavaScript/Node.js, vm.runInNewContext can be escaped; in PHP, eval() enables arbitrary code execution. This is often the most directly exploitable injection vulnerability as it requires no intermediate interpreter.

name: Improper Control of Filename for Include/Require Statement in PHP (File Inclusion)
description: File inclusion vulnerabilities occur when PHP applications use user-controllable input in include(), require(), include_once(), or require_once() statements. Local File Inclusion (LFI) allows attackers to include files from the local server filesystem, potentially reading sensitive configuration files, PHP session files, or log files. Remote File Inclusion (RFI) allows including files from remote servers, directly enabling remote code execution. While primarily a PHP issue, similar vulnerabilities exist in other languages with dynamic file inclusion. LFI can be escalated to RCE through various techniques including log poisoning, PHP filter chains, zip/phar wrapper abuse, and /proc/self/environ injection. Prevention requires avoiding user input in file inclusion paths, using allowlists for includable files, and disabling allow_url_include in PHP configuration.

name: Insecure Operation on Windows Junction / Mount Point
description: This weakness occurs when software performs file operations that follow Windows NTFS junctions or mount points without validating that the target is within the expected directory. NTFS junctions are similar to Unix symbolic links but operate at the directory level. Attackers create junctions that redirect file operations to unintended locations, such as redirecting a temporary directory to point to a privileged system directory. When a privileged application writes to or deletes files in the junction target, it performs those operations with its elevated privileges on the attacker-chosen location. This is a common privilege escalation technique on Windows and has been used in numerous local privilege escalation exploits against antivirus software, Windows services, and installation programs. Prevention requires checking for junction points before file operations, using restricted create options, and operating with minimum necessary privileges.

name: Insecure Temporary File
description: This weakness occurs when software creates temporary files in an insecure manner, potentially allowing unauthorized access, modification, or creation of symlinks to redirect file operations. Common issues include using predictable filenames that enable race condition attacks, creating temp files in world-writable directories without exclusive access flags, not setting restrictive permissions, and not cleaning up temporary files that may contain sensitive data. Attackers can pre-create files with the predicted name (symlink to sensitive files), race the file creation to inject content, or read temporary files containing sensitive data after they are created. Prevention requires using language-provided secure temporary file APIs (mkstemp, tempfile.NamedTemporaryFile), creating files with exclusive flags (O_EXCL), setting restrictive permissions, and cleaning up files promptly after use.

name: Improper Link Resolution Before File Access (Link Following)
description: This weakness occurs when software accesses files through symbolic links (symlinks) or hard links without verifying that the link target is within the expected directory or is an appropriate target. Attackers create symlinks that redirect file operations to unintended locations. A classic attack involves creating a symlink in /tmp pointing to /etc/passwd; when a privileged program writes to the temp file, it overwrites the password file instead. Link following attacks are a primary technique for local privilege escalation on Unix/Linux systems, particularly against setuid programs and system services. Similar attacks on Windows use NTFS junctions and mount points (CWE-1386). Prevention requires checking for symlinks before operations (lstat), using O_NOFOLLOW when opening files, operating in restricted directories, and using privilege separation.

name: Observable Discrepancy (Timing Side Channel)
description: This weakness occurs when software exhibits observable differences in behavior (timing, error messages, response content) that allow attackers to infer sensitive information. Timing side channels are the most exploited variant: comparing a user-supplied password byte-by-byte with a stored password and returning early on the first mismatch leaks information about which bytes are correct, enabling character-by-character recovery. Similar timing discrepancies in cryptographic operations enabled attacks like BEAST, Lucky13, and various padding oracle attacks against TLS. Beyond timing, observable discrepancies include different error messages for valid vs invalid usernames (enabling user enumeration), different response sizes or HTTP status codes, and cache timing differences that leak information about data access patterns. Prevention requires constant-time comparison functions for secrets, uniform error responses regardless of failure type, and elimination of data-dependent branching in cryptographic code.

name: Observable Timing Discrepancy
description: This is a specific case of observable discrepancy (CWE-203) focused on timing variations that leak sensitive information. Software that takes measurably different amounts of time to process valid vs invalid inputs, correct vs incorrect credentials, or existent vs non-existent users creates a timing oracle that attackers can exploit. Network-level timing measurements may seem noisy but statistical techniques with sufficient samples reliably extract the signal. Concurrency-based techniques (sending requests in parallel and comparing completion order) further improve timing attack accuracy. Critical areas requiring constant-time implementation include password verification, HMAC comparison, cryptographic operations, authorization checks, and token validation. Languages and frameworks provide constant-time comparison functions (hmac.compare_digest in Python, crypto.timingSafeEqual in Node.js, MessageDigest.isEqual in Java) that should be used for all secret comparisons.
